title: Appendix A — Milestone 1: Proposal + Dataset
text:
After creating their GitHub repository using the link in Where to Submit section, each team should use the proposal.qmd file to submit their project proposal as an appendix in their project website. The proposal should include the followings Github: https://github.com/mac-stat212-s26/project-metrotransit-rishika-hadley
Transit access in the Twin Cities team member names - Rishika Kundu, Hadley Wilkins and Laurice Jimu a short description of the project - We are looking at metro transit spatial data of their trips and the income levels of the of the areas they are coming from. 2-3 broad research questions the team is interested in exploring - Is there a correlation between income and ridership, how many stops in richer areas vs poorer ones, where is there a need for more lines or buses the reasons/inspirations behind choosing the topic - We are passionate about metro transit and to see how transit is serving different communities one potential data set that can be used to answer the research questions - https://gisdata.mn.gov/dataset/us-mn-state-metc-trans-transit-count-headway-sum communication plan, ie, primary communication channel (eg, Google Chat, Slack, Email), expected response time (eg, 24 hours during the week), meeting time (eg, “we will meet 7-8pm every Wednesday”) - Text groupchat - every day we chat, Meeting time is during class and we text about things where we need help roles–everyone is expected to contribute to the project but having leads helps with accountability. In particular, determine (1) point of contact–the main person to email the instructor/preceptors, - Laurice Jimu (2) technical lead–the person who will oversee the code base and website, - Rishika Kundu (3) style lead–the person who will make sure the code is following the COMP/STAT 112/212: Code Styling Guide - hadley Wilkins conflict resolution, ie, if a team member misses a deadline, how will the team handle it? How will you make decisions if the team is split 50/50 on a something? - we will talk about it as responsible adults , we can’t be 5050 because there are three of us, also we are really similar and so chances of conflict are low a rough implementation plan, ie, list of tasks with due date and roles–refer to the list of deliverables when building your list. The plan should be added as GitHub Issues within the project repository–see the callout box below for details. - Please create for me:
To track your project progress, utilize the GitHub Issues feature by creating a set of milestones and tasks (issues in GitHub terminology) inside each. Below are the instructions for how to add a task into a milestone. Go to your project GitHub repository on the web Select Issues tab Click Milestones –> click New milestone –> type a title, pick a due date, and type a description then hit Create milestone Click New issue –> type a tile, add a description, assign members (from the right list), and hit Create Repeat the steps above to add new milestones and new tasks.
